---
title: "Import Data"
---

LIS and LWS datasets are split by country, year, and level (household and individual). To load data in LISSY, we can use the following two methods. The most recently developed `lissyuse` function, or the previous in-use method `read.LIS()` that it still being used to import files less often used like the ones for the replicate weights in LWS. 

For the remainder of this guide, we will focus on Method 1, which imports the selected LIS data into a list, where each element corresponds to a dataset for a specific country–year combination.


## Method 1: `lissyuse`

Using the `lissyuse()` function from our in-house [lissyrtools](https://lis-cross-national-data-center.github.io/lissyrtools/) package has two main advantages:

1.  Imports all datasets available, given specific ISO-2 country codes by default;

2.  Automatically selects the correct data level based on the variables requested;

```{r, echo=TRUE , message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
```

<br>

### Advantage 1: Import entire country series



LIS data is stored separately for each country and year, identified by a <b>ccyy</b> country-year code. This function allows users to import an entire country series by specifying its ISO-2 country code, with optional year range parameters to filter for a specific time frame. 

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold'}

#' We can load all the datasets available in LIS 
#'  for Mexico and the USA, simply by inputting their ISO-2 codes.

lis_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("mx", "us"), 
  vars = "dhi",
)

# The output of `lissyrtools::lissyuse()` will always be a list of data frames. 
class(lis_data)
names(lis_data)
```



```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold' }

#' For a shorter time span, 
#'  the user can also specify the `from` and `to` arguments.

lis_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("mx", "us"), 
  vars = "dhi",
  from = 2014,
  to = 2016
)
```

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold' }

# Specific datasets can also be selected by specifying its `ccyy` code

lis_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("it16", "mx", "us"), 
  vars = "dhi",
  from = 2014,
  to = 2016
)

```

<br>

### Advantage 2: p-level variable... p-level data ! 



The second benefit of using `lissyuse()` is that it automatically imports datasets at the level determined by the variables selected. If the users selects only household-level varaibles in the `vars` argument such as: disposable household income (<b>dhi</b>), geographic region (<b>region_c</b>) or capital income (<b>hicapital</b>), the data will be loadad at the household level. As soon as the user includes an individual-level variable, such as gender (<b>sex</b>) or labour force status (<b>lfs</b>), the dataset level adjusts accordingly.


```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold'}

#' Based on the variables defined in the `vars` argument, `lissyuse()` will 
#'  select the level of the datasets in accordance.  

#' Names of the data frames in the list will have an `h` suffix.

h_level_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = "lu18", 
  vars = c("dhi", "region_c", "typehh") # only h-level 
)


```

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold' }

# Names of the data frames in the list will have a `p` suffix. 

p_level_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = "lu18", 
  vars = c("age", "sex", "relation", "emp", "pi11") # only p-level 
)


```


```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, results = 'hold'}

#' If both h-level and p-level variables are selected, then `lissyuse()` will
#'   automatically merge both files in one single data frame. 

#' Names of the data frames will NOT have a suffix.

merged_hhd_ind_data <- lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = "lu18", 
  vars = c("dhi", "region_c", "typehh", "age", "sex", "relation", "emp", "pi11")
)



#' NOTE: some variables are always imported by default, 
#'  with no need to be defined explicitly. 
names(merged_hhd_ind_data[[1]])
```


<br>

### Importing LWS data

To import LWS data, one would need to change the selection in the <b>Project</b> drop-down menu, and set the `lws` logical argument equal to `TRUE`.

![](/images/project_lws.png){fig-align="center"}

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE}
us_lws_data = lissyrtools::lissyuse(
  data = c("us"), 
  vars = c("dnw"),  # disposable net wealth - LWS specific variable. 
  from = 2015, 
  lws = TRUE        # `lws` logical argument  
)

names(us_lws_data[[1]])
```
